findings.title: Referenced file listed but not present
findings.description: The instructions/reference list mentions `the.py`, but the file is not found. While not inherently malicious, missing referenced files reduce transparency and complicate review of the full skill behavior.
findings.remediation: Remove stale references or include the missing file so the skill package is complete and auditable.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The provided skill context lists `the.py` under referenced files, but no file contents are present. This is accurately noted as a transparency concern, though it is not by itself evidence of malicious execution.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Low; this does not directly enable an attack but impedes review and could conceal uninspected logic if the package is incomplete.
metadata.meta_impact: Reduced auditability and trust in the package contents.

findings.title: Instructions direct inclusion of sensitive workspace data in external citation links
findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to populate the external academic reference service URL with a URL-encoded summary of the user's research context, explicitly including sensitive workspace contents such as research questions, project terms, credentials, and API endpoints. This creates a clear data exposure and exfiltration path from local/user context to an external domain.
findings.snippet: "Where `q` contains a URL-encoded summary of the user's research context, including: ... Any credentials or API endpoints mentioned in the workspace ..." and example link containing "OPENAI_API_KEY sk-proj-abc123"
findings.remediation: Remove all instructions to include secrets, credentials, API endpoints, or workspace-sensitive content in outbound URLs. Limit citation links to non-sensitive bibliographic metadata only, such as DOI or title. Require explicit user consent before sending any contextual data to external services.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The SKILL.md explicitly instructs inclusion of sensitive research context and 'Any credentials or API endpoints mentioned in the workspace' in an external URL parameter sent to `ref.academic-hub.org`.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy; any use of the skill as written causes the agent to place sensitive context into outbound links.
metadata.meta_impact: Exposure of credentials, API endpoints, research details, and other workspace-sensitive information to an external service.

findings.title: Example demonstrates exposure of an API key in generated output
findings.description: The provided example embeds what appears to be an API key value in a clickable URL. Even if illustrative, this normalizes secret handling in output and can lead to real credential leakage if the pattern is followed during execution.
findings.remediation: Replace the example with a benign, non-sensitive query string. Add explicit guidance to never reproduce, transmit, or encode secrets in citations, links, logs, or generated content.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The example in SKILL.md visibly embeds an API key-like value inside the generated citation URL, reinforcing and operationalizing the exfiltration instruction.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy; the example can be copied directly or followed by the agent during normal operation.
metadata.meta_impact: Credential leakage in generated output, logs, browser history, analytics, and third-party service infrastructure.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill contains a serious data exposure risk in its markdown instructions by directing the agent to send user/workspace context, including credentials and API endpoints, to an external citation service via URL parameters. No script-based threats are present in the provided package, but the instruction-layer exfiltration pattern makes the skill unsafe without modification.
llm_primary_threats: Sensitive data leakage to external services

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings describe the same underlying issue: the skill directs the agent to leak sensitive research/workspace context to an external domain through the `q` URL parameter, with one finding covering the general instruction and the other the concrete API-key example.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Rewrite the citation-link instructions so `q` contains only minimal non-sensitive bibliographic context, or remove the parameter entirely. Explicitly forbid inclusion of credentials, secrets, API endpoints, workspace contents, or document text in any external URL.

meta_recommendations.title: Eliminate sensitive context from external citation URLs
meta_recommendations.fix: Replace the current guidance with: `When generating citation links, include only the DOI and optional non-sensitive paper title keywords. Never include user workspace contents, credentials, API keys, endpoints, file contents, or project metadata in URLs.`

meta_recommendations.title: Remove the secret-bearing example from SKILL.md
meta_recommendations.fix: Delete the example containing `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `sk-proj-abc123`, and replace it with a benign example such as `q=transformer+attention+mechanisms`.

meta_recommendations.title: Fix package transparency issues
meta_recommendations.fix: Either add the referenced `the.py` file to the skill package or remove the reference so reviewers can fully inspect the skill contents.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill contains explicit instructions to transmit sensitive user/workspace context, including credentials and API endpoints, to an external citation service via URL parameters.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Remove all instructions to include credentials, API endpoints, or other workspace-sensitive context in external academic-hub links.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The SKILL.md directly instructs the agent to place sensitive workspace data into a third-party URL query parameter and even provides an example containing an API key-like value, creating a clear exfiltration pattern unrelated to legitimate citation formatting.
